name: Test Driven Development
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in Test Driven Development as a competency should be able to explain the TDD mindset and red-green-refactor cycle, distinguish TDD from test-after unit testing, and write simple unit tests using a mainstream framework (e.g., JUnit, NUnit, pytest, Jest, RSpec) in their primary language. They should structure tests using AAA, keep tests small and focused on single behaviours, and apply basic assertion libraries. They can use simple test doubles (stubs, fakes, mocks) to isolate units, avoid flaky tests by controlling state and dependencies, and recognize the difference between unit, integration, and end-to-end tests. They understand how TDD drives design, identify when code is hard to test, and perform basic refactoring for testability. They can organize test files, follow naming conventions, and run tests locally and in CI pipelines (e.g., GitHub Actions, Jenkins). They know how to interpret test failures, use build tools to run tests, and integrate code coverage tools at a basic level. They collaborate with teammates on TDD practices, review tests for clarity, and document or communicate testing approaches. They maintain test suite reliability, avoid duplication, and keep tests readable as living documentation. They can set up local environments for consistent test runs and ensure tests are included in version control and CI, supporting fast feedback and regression safety.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core TDD Principles and Mindset","scope_included":"- Understands Test Driven Development as a software design and development approach, not only a testing technique
- Applies the core TDD loop: writing a failing test first, making it pass with minimal code, then refactoring while keeping tests green
- Recognizes the benefits TDD aims to achieve: fast feedback, improved design, regression safety, and enabling confident refactoring
- Distinguishes clearly between TDD and “test-after” unit testing, and can explain why test-first impacts design differently
- Understands the concept of incrementalism in TDD: making very small, safe steps instead of large untested changes
- Appreciates that tests are part of the codebase and should follow the same quality standards as production code
- Recognizes that TDD focuses primarily on unit-level behaviour and design, even though tests exist at other levels
- Understands the relationship between TDD and code quality concepts such as low coupling, high cohesion, and single responsibility
- Understands that TDD does not guarantee fewer defects on its own; its value depends on disciplined practice and good test design
- Knows that TDD is compatible with various development processes (Scrum, Kanban, XP, etc.) and is not tied to a single methodology","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Red–Green–Refactor Cycle","scope_included":"- Follows the TDD cycle strictly in simple and moderately complex tasks: write failing test (red), write minimal code to pass (green), refactor while keeping tests passing (refactor)
- Keeps the red phase narrow and focused, writing one test at a time that expresses a small, concrete behaviour
- Writes only enough production code in the green phase to make the current failing test pass, avoiding speculative implementation
- Uses the refactor phase to improve code structure and test code, without changing observable behaviour
- Understands that refactoring is safer when the test suite is reliable and fast
- Identifies when a test is doing too much and splits it into multiple tests following the TDD cycle
- Avoids skipping directly from red to elaborate design without passing through a minimal green step
- Practices keeping the time between red and green short (minutes, not hours) to maintain tight feedback loops
- Recognizes when prolonged red state indicates that the test is too large or the design is unclear and should be narrowed
- Uses the TDD cycle to drive incremental implementation of small features or components end-to-end at the unit level","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Unit Testing Foundations in a TDD Context","scope_included":"- Writes unit tests that focus on a single unit of behaviour (method, function, or small component), keeping tests small and focused
- Understands and applies common properties of good unit tests (such as FIRST: Fast, Independent, Repeatable, Self-checking, Timely)
- Distinguishes unit tests from integration, system, and end-to-end tests based on scope and dependencies
- Writes tests that are deterministic and do not depend on external state, time, or environment where avoidable
- Validates both expected outputs and externally visible side effects of a unit’s behaviour
- Understands the Arrange–Act–Assert (AAA) structure and uses it consistently in unit tests
- Uses assertion libraries effectively to clearly express expected outcomes and failure conditions
- Understands positive (happy path) and negative (error/edge case) tests and includes both when driving design
- Recognizes flaky tests and basic causes (time-dependence, shared mutable state, network calls) and knows to avoid these in TDD unit tests
- Uses basic techniques to make tests robust: controlling time, resetting state between tests, avoiding global/static mutable state
- Understands that tests should fail when behaviour is incorrect and pass when correct, and avoids tests that always pass or always fail","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Test Design and Structure for TDD","scope_included":"- Designs tests starting from externally visible behaviour and contracts, not internal implementation details
- Writes tests with clear, intention-revealing names that describe the behaviour under test and conditions
- Structures test classes and files logically around units or behaviours (e.g., one test class per production class or feature)
- Keeps tests focused, each verifying a single behaviour or scenario, avoiding multiple unrelated assertions in one test where possible
- Uses data-driven or parameterized tests for straightforward variations on the same behaviour when supported by the chosen framework
- Identifies and covers important edge cases for simple business logic (boundary values, null/empty, invalid inputs, special cases)
- Understands the concept of the “test pyramid” and places most tests at the unit level in the context of TDD
- Avoids unnecessary duplication in test setups by using setup/teardown mechanisms provided by the test framework, while keeping setups readable
- Recognizes when a test is too dependent on internal implementation (over-specified tests) and can adjust it to focus on observable behaviour
- Keeps test code clear and readable, acknowledging that tests are a form of living documentation for system behaviour
- Uses test naming and structure conventions of the team or ecosystem (e.g., `should_doX_when_Y`, `Given_When_Then` style) consistently","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Driving Design with Tests","scope_included":"- Uses TDD to iteratively discover APIs and method signatures based on the needs expressed by tests
- Lets tests guide decisions about input validation, output structure, and basic error-handling behaviour
- Identifies when tests indicate that a unit is doing too much (difficult to test) and performs basic refactoring to split responsibilities
- Understands that difficulty in testing often signals design issues, such as excessive coupling or hidden responsibilities
- Recognizes when introducing small abstractions (interfaces, helper classes, pure functions) can make code easier to test and maintain
- Uses constructor or method injection to make dependencies testable instead of hard-coding them in the implementation
- For simple domains, allows tests to drive domain model evolution (e.g., value objects, simple aggregates, or basic entities)
- Uses TDD to avoid premature optimization, focusing first on correctness and clarity of behaviour
- Applies simple refactoring patterns in response to tests (extract method, introduce parameter, inline variable, rename for clarity)
- Ensures that refactoring does not change test expectations, using failing tests as a signal of unintended behavioural change
- Understands that tests should be resilient to reasonable refactoring of implementation details, reinforcing behaviour-focused design","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Basic Use of Test Doubles and Isolation","scope_included":"- Understands the role of test doubles in TDD: isolating the unit under test from slow, unstable, or complex external dependencies
- Distinguishes between basic categories of test doubles (fake, stub, mock, spy, dummy) at a conceptual level
- Uses stubs or simple fakes to replace external services such as file systems, HTTP clients, or databases in unit tests
- Introduces test doubles only when needed to keep unit tests fast, deterministic, and focused on the unit’s behaviour
- Avoids overusing mocks for purely internal implementation details that do not represent meaningful behaviour or contracts
- Uses assertion capabilities (or mocking tools) to verify that important interactions occurred (e.g., method called with expected arguments)
- Resets or recreates test doubles per test to ensure isolation and avoid interference between tests
- Recognizes the trade-off between interaction-based tests (verifying calls) and state-based tests (verifying outcomes) and prefers state-based where practical
- Avoids tightly coupling tests to the exact sequence of calls when that sequence is not part of the stable contract
- Uses simple hand-written test doubles where mocking frameworks would add unnecessary complexity for basic scenarios","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Working with Existing Code Using TDD Principles","scope_included":"- Recognizes when existing code is not testable and identifies barriers such as global state, hidden dependencies, or large methods
- Applies a “characterization tests first” mindset when modifying untested legacy code, capturing current behaviour before making changes
- Wraps complex or legacy units with higher-level tests when fine-grained unit tests are initially difficult, gradually improving testability
- Performs small, safe refactorings (e.g., extract method, introduce parameter, break long methods) to make units more amenable to TDD in future changes
- Uses dependency injection patterns (constructor, setter, or method parameters) to replace hard-coded dependencies with testable collaborators
- Avoids extensive rewrites without tests; instead, incrementally adds tests around changed behaviour
- Identifies seams in code (places where behaviour can be altered without modifying the code, such as interfaces or configuration) to enable testing
- Understands that in legacy contexts it may not always be possible to follow pure TDD, but still applies TDD principles where feasible
- Uses refactoring tools and IDE support to safely restructure code under test coverage
- Communicates limitations of applying TDD on heavily coupled legacy components and suggests incremental improvement strategies","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Quality and Maintainability of the Test Suite","scope_included":"- Keeps the test suite fast enough to run frequently on a local machine during development
- Ensures tests are reliable and not flaky; investigates and fixes root causes when intermittent failures occur
- Avoids duplicating similar tests and refactors overlapping tests into clearer, minimal sets of scenarios
- Identifies and removes or corrects obsolete tests when behaviour or requirements change
- Maintains a consistent level of abstraction within each test, avoiding mixing very low-level and high-level details in a single test
- Keeps test setup code readable and avoids overly complicated fixture hierarchies that obscure test intent
- Uses meaningful test data that makes behaviours and edge cases clear rather than opaque numeric or string literals
- Recognizes when the test suite provides insufficient coverage of important behaviours and adds tests where gaps exist
- Avoids writing extremely brittle tests that depend on nonessential aspects of implementation (logging messages, internal ordering when not part of spec)
- Applies code review feedback to improve the clarity, structure, and usefulness of tests
- Treats failing tests as important signals rather than nuisances and works to keep the test suite in a consistently passing state on the main branch","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Integrating TDD into Daily Development Workflow","scope_included":"- Uses TDD when implementing new small-to-medium features or bug fixes, at least on core units of logic
- Plans development tasks by thinking first about behaviours to test, then about implementation steps
- Runs unit tests frequently during development, especially before commits and pushes
- Uses version control practices that complement TDD, such as small commits aligned with passing tests and incremental changes
- Recognizes tasks or areas where TDD is especially beneficial (complex business rules, calculations, transformation logic)
- Identifies situations where strict TDD may be harder to apply (UI-heavy work, tightly coupled legacy code) and adapts while still writing tests
- Monitors how long the test suite takes to run locally and makes adjustments to keep the feedback loop quick
- Collaborates with teammates to agree on when and where TDD will be applied on a given project or module
- Uses tests to validate bug reports: first reproducing the bug with a failing test, then implementing the fix to turn the test green
- Incorporates TDD tasks into personal estimates and planning, acknowledging the initial overhead and long-term gains","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Tooling and Frameworks for TDD","scope_included":"- Uses at least one unit testing framework fluently within the primary language or stack (e.g., JUnit/JUnit 5 for Java, NUnit or xUnit.net for .NET, pytest or unittest for Python, Jest or Mocha for JavaScript/TypeScript, RSpec or Minitest for Ruby, JUnit-like frameworks for Kotlin, Go’s testing package)
- Understands the basic structure of tests as defined by the chosen framework (test annotations/attributes, naming conventions, setup/teardown methods)
- Uses assertion libraries provided by the framework and understands common assertion patterns (equality, truth, exceptions, collection contents)
- Uses basic mocking or test double libraries when appropriate for the stack (e.g., Mockito for Java, Moq for .NET, unittest.mock for Python, Sinon for JavaScript)
- Configures test discovery and execution within the IDE or editor (e.g., IntelliJ, VS Code, Visual Studio, Eclipse, JetBrains Rider)
- Runs subsets of the test suite (single test, test class, test file, or tagged tests) to support rapid TDD cycles
- Interprets test failure output from the framework, including stack traces and assertion messages, to quickly locate and fix issues
- Uses build tools to run tests from the command line (e.g., Maven/Gradle for Java, dotnet CLI for .NET, npm/yarn for JavaScript, pytest CLI for Python, rake or bundle exec for Ruby)
- Integrates code coverage tooling at a basic level to see which lines and branches are exercised by tests (e.g., JaCoCo, Coverlet, Istanbul/nyc, coverage.py, SimpleCov)
- Recognizes the difference between high coverage and meaningful coverage and avoids chasing coverage numbers without behavioural value
- Uses or understands basic test grouping, tagging, or categorization mechanisms (e.g., JUnit tags, pytest markers, Jest test suites) to manage test runs","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Code Review, and Communication in a TDD Context","scope_included":"- Discusses TDD expectations and practices with teammates and aligns on the level of TDD to apply in a given project
- Explains the intent of tests and the behaviours they cover to peers during code reviews or pairing sessions
- Reviews others’ test code for clarity, correctness, and adherence to team conventions, providing constructive feedback
- Participates in pair programming or mob programming sessions where TDD is practiced collectively
- Uses shared vocabulary around TDD (red-green-refactor, test pyramid, unit vs integration, test double types) in team discussions
- Documents known testing strategies for modules or services (e.g., which parts are covered by unit TDD, which by integration tests)
- Raises concerns when new features or refactors are not adequately covered by tests and suggests where TDD could help
- Understands and respects team agreements around when tests are required before merging changes
- Helps less experienced developers understand the basic TDD cycle and how to get started, by walking through simple examples
- Communicates test-related trade-offs clearly (e.g., when adding a test at a different level is more appropriate than strict unit-level TDD)","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Documentation, Conventions, and Test Organization","scope_included":"- Follows existing project or team conventions for organizing test files, folders, and naming (e.g., `src/test/java`, `tests/`, `*_test` files)
- Documents any non-obvious test setups or fixtures so others can understand and safely modify tests
- Uses comments sparingly in tests, relying primarily on clear naming and structure, but adds comments where behaviour or rationale is subtle
- Maintains or updates lightweight documentation describing how to run tests locally and what the main test suites are
- Ensures new tests are included in any documented or scripted test commands used by the team
- Adheres to agreed-upon standards for test naming, including behaviour descriptions and input/expected-output patterns
- Organizes tests so that related behaviours and features are grouped logically, aiding discoverability
- Updates or adds to simple testing guidelines for the team when recurring issues are discovered (e.g., flaky tests causes, mocking pitfalls)
- Uses test code as living documentation and ensures that tests remain readable over time as requirements evolve
- Keeps documentation aligned with current TDD and test practices, updating it when tools or frameworks change","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Environment Setup and Continuous Integration Basics for TDD","scope_included":"- Sets up local development environments so that tests run reliably across machines (consistent tooling versions, dependencies, configurations)
- Ensures that necessary test resources (configuration files, test data, environment variables) are included in version control or clearly provisioned
- Integrates unit test execution into the project’s build pipeline (e.g., as part of Maven/Gradle build, dotnet build/test, npm test scripts)
- Understands how continuous integration (CI) servers run tests on each commit or pull request (e.g., GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, Azure DevOps, CircleCI)
- Configures or updates basic CI pipelines to include unit test steps and to fail builds when tests fail
- Reads and interprets CI test reports and logs to diagnose issues that appear only in CI environments
- Works with the team to ensure that tests used in TDD locally also run in CI with consistent results
- Understands the benefits of keeping the CI test suite relatively fast, especially unit tests used in TDD
- Uses CI status on branches and pull requests as a gate for merging changes, ensuring tests are always passing on main branches
- Helps keep environment-related flakiness low by standardizing how tests depend on system resources, paths, and configuration","is_non_core_section":true}]}
